data.title: Cemtrex. announces $2M registered direct offering
data.description:
• Cemtrex (NASDAQ:CETX) announced on Thursday that it has entered into a definitive agreement for the purchase of approximately 0.67M shares with a single institutional investor, at a purchase price of $3 per share, with gross proceeds expected to be $2M.
  • The transaction is expected to close on or about Dec 11, 2025, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.
  • The company expects to use the net proceeds from the offering for general corporate purposes, including working capital and potential acquisitions.
  • The offering is being made pursuant to an effective shelf registration statement with the SEC and declared effective on February 3, 2025.

data.title: C3 AI receives FedRAMP authorization, opening the door for government agency adoption
data.description:
C3 AI (AI) has received FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) authorization, which opens the door for government agencies to readily adopt its enterprise artificial intelligence software platform, the company announced today.

C3 AI shares ticked up 1% during Thursday morning market action.

"Obtaining FedRAMP authorization underlines that the C3 Agentic AI Platform is trusted, secure and compliant with the stringent standards that federal agencies demand," said C3 AI CEO Stephen Ehikian. "We're proud to deliver to government customers the same Enterprise AI capabilities used by commercial enterprises — now with full federal-grade accreditation."

The authorization has made C3 AI available on the FedRAMP Marketplace.

data.title: Ciena soars on strong Q4 results, guidance
data.description:
Ciena (CIEN) shares jumped more than 5% on Thursday after the networking systems provider reported better-than-expected results and issued strong guidance for the coming quarter and fiscal year. 

For the period ending Nov. 1, Ciena said it earned an adjusted $0.91 per share as revenue rose 20.5% year-over-year to come in at $1.35B. Three customers accounted for 43.6% of total revenue in the quarter, while two customers accounted for 28.4% of revenue for the full-year. Analysts were expecting the company to earn $0.77 per share on $1.29B in revenue.

“Our record fiscal fourth quarter and full-year performance reinforces our position as the global leader in high-speed connectivity with an expanding role in the AI ecosystem,” said Gary Smith, president and CEO, Ciena, in a statement. “Looking ahead, we are confident in our growth trajectory over the coming years, driven by durable demand from our cloud and service provider customers and a growing set of opportunities inside and around the data center.”

Looking to the first-quarter of fiscal 2026, Ciena said it expects to generate between $1.35B and $1.43B in revenue, well above the $1.25B analysts were expecting. Adjusted gross margins are expected to be between 43% and 44%.

For the full-year, sales are forecast to be between $5.7B and $6.1B, also above the $5.53B estimate. Adjusted gross margin is expected to be 43%, plus or minus 1%. The company also said on its earnings call that operating margin is expected to improve and be around 17%, plus or minus 1%. 

Capital expenditures will be between $250M and $275M, aided to support the robust demand and costs for 3 nm mask sets, Ciena added.

The company added it expects to buy back around $330M in stock in fiscal 2026.

(This story has been updated to include conference call information.)

data.title: Nasdaq and S&P 500 fall as Oracle’s post-earnings drop weighs on markets
data.description:
Wall Street trades mixed on Thursday as the tech-oriented Nasdaq Composite and S&P 500 drag lower after Oracle (ORCL) posted mixed results, pressuring the AI trade.

The Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) tumbled the hardest as it lost 1.1%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) pushed down by 0.5% while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) climbed up by 0.4%.

Across sectors, six of the 11 S&P groups are in positive territory led by Materials. At the other end of the spectrum, Info Tech has been the weakest performer so far.

Investors also continue to react to Wednesday’s Fed decision to cut its policy rate by 25 basis points to 3.50%-3.75%, which is now its lowest level in three years. 

“The Federal Reserve sent a clear message that the Fed is in the midst of an accommodative phase, largely driven by the recent weakness in the labor market. While 2026 may not bring as many rate cuts as 2025, it's clear that the Fed's prior hiking cycle that disrupted markets and the economy post-Covid is firmly in the rearview mirror, and that's bullish for stocks,” Chris Kampitsis, managing partner of Barnum Financial Group said. 

Furthermore, Deutsche Bank’s Jim Reid stated: “Bond yields have also moved lower, which partly reflects the Fed and the wider risk-off tone this morning.” Reid noted Japan’s robust bond auction and Australia’s unexpected employment contraction as contributing factors. 

Yields were lower across the curve, with the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) falling 3 basis points to 3.51% and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dropping 4 basis points to 4.11%. 

As for stocks that were on the move, Oracle (ORCL) dropped 14.5% while shares of Mosaic (MOS) pushed up by 5.7%.

data.title: September wholesale inventories grow by more than expected
data.description: Wholesale inventories increased 0.5% M/M to $911.5B in September, stronger than the 0.1% increase expected and August's flat reading, according to data released by the U.S. Census Bureau on Thursday.

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: PL, ORCL, GEMI, and more
data.description:
Stock futures edged lower on Thursday morning even after the Federal Reserve delivered a widely anticipated 25-basis-point rate cut on Wednesday, its third reduction this year, taking the federal funds target range down to 3.5%–3.75%, the lowest level in three years.

Here are some of Thursday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Planet Labs (PL) +22% - Shares jumped after a healthy third-quarter earnings report and revenue growth expectations. Planet expects fourth-quarter revenue to be in the range of approximately $76 million to $80 million, compared to the $73.55 million that analysts forecast. Compared to the previous quarter, guidance for both Q4 and the full year has increased, reflecting contract wins and improved visibility, along with a focus on sustaining the Q4 revenue growth rate into fiscal '27. 

  • Gemini Space Station (GEMI) +6.5%—Shares spiked after its derivatives arm, Titan, secured a designated contract market license from the CFTC, clearing the way to offer prediction markets to U.S. customers through yes/no event contracts covering topics from sports to economic data. The company said trading will soon be available on its web interface and later on mobile, while Titan also plans to expand into crypto futures, options, and perpetual contracts as part of Gemini’s ambition to build a “one-stop financial super app.” President Cameron Winklevoss said the approval reflects Acting Chair Pham’s pro-innovation stance and the CFTC’s support for emerging markets.
  • Enova International (ENVA) +6.4% - Shares rose after the company announced plans to acquire Grasshopper Bancorp and its wholly owned subsidiary Grasshopper Bank N.A. in a cash and stock transaction valued at approximately $369 million. The transaction is expected to generate adjusted earnings per share accretion of more than 15% within the first year and more than 25% once the synergies are fully realized beyond the first year.
     

Biggest stock losers

  • Oxford Industries (OXM) -24% - Shares tumbled after the company cut its full-year sales and profit outlook despite a solid Q3, citing tariff-related uncertainties as the main headwind. The company now expects FY2025 net sales of $1.47B–$1.49B vs. $1.52B last year and the $1.49B consensus, and it projects a GAAP loss of $1.52 to $1.32 per share due to $61M in noncash Johnny Was impairment charges, compared with earnings of $5.87 per share in 2024. Adjusted EPS is seen at $2.20–$2.40, down from $6.68 in FY2024 and below the $2.90 consensus. CEO Thomas Chubb said early Q4 trends point to a softer holiday season driven by tariff-related product constraints and tougher comps.
  • Oracle (ORCL) -15% – Shares plunged despite the company delivering mixed FQ2 results, with revenue rising 14% Y/Y on the back of 36% growth in cloud and a 66% surge in infrastructure revenue. Principal Financial Officer Doug Kehring noted that GAAP EPS jumped 91% to $2.10 and non-GAAP EPS rose 54% to $2.26, lifted by a $2.7B pre-tax gain from selling its stake in Ampere. Remaining Performance Obligations climbed 15% sequentially to $523B, supported by new commitments from Meta, NVIDIA, and others.

data.title: Evertz Technologies Limited raises dividend by 2.5% to CAD 0.205
data.description:
• Evertz Technologies Limited (ET:CA) declares CAD 0.205/share quarterly dividend, 2.5% increase from prior dividend of CAD 0.200.
  • Evertz Board of Directors also declared a special dividend of $1.00.
  • Both payable Dec. 24; for shareholders of record Dec. 18; ex-div Dec. 18.
  • See ET:CA Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Lion Group Holding buys 88.49 Bitcoin for $8 million
data.description:
• Lion Group Holding (LGHL) on Thursday announced the acquisition of 88.49 units of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) for $8 million.
  • This move underscores LGHL's commitment to positioning Bitcoin as a core reserve asset within its portfolio.
  • LGHL remains committed to exploring strategic opportunities within the digital asset space and will provide periodic updates as it continues to grow and manage its treasury reserve.
  • Lion Group Holding (LGHL) shares are down 1.6% in morning trade, in line with the broader crypto market.

data.title: Truist, Citizens, First Horizon lose bull rates at Baird after recent climb
data.description:
Baird Equity Research on Thursday downgraded Truist Financial (TFC), Citizens Financial Group (CFG) and First Horizon (FHN) shares to Neutral from Outperform on the premise that the regional banking group's recent rally limits upside potential.

The three stocks each rose 10%-12% from a month ago, aligning with the broader State Street SPDR S&P Regional Banking ETF's (KRE) 10% rise, and easily outpacing the overall S&P 500 Index's 0.5% gain.

That relative outperformance suggests "a more balanced risk/reward across the broader bank group from our perspective," analyst David George wrote in a note. 

Fundamentally, though, George noted that preprovision net revenue trends remain healthy, "credit is in good shape, and banks are getting more aggressive in terms of capital management," citing bank updates this week.

Minutes after the opening bell, TFC gained 0.6%, CFG +0.5% and FHN -0.8%.

Seeking Alpha's peer page shows various metrics comparing the three lenders.
